sections.content: (B.2.2). The full inventory of 94 released mocks is enumerated in Table 3. synthetically; (iii) outbound network calls are replaced by in-process state mutations. Where the reference application exposes capabilities that depend on these stripped layers (e.g., real-time push notifications, cross-account sharing, payment processing), the mock instead exposes deterministic stub that supports task verification without network dependence. B.2.3 Trademark, Trade Dress, and Asset Compliance The mock identifiers used in Table 3 and Figure 10 (e.g., slack, gmail, shopify_admin) are internal development labels that pin each synthesis target to its real-world reference for grounding the Plan and Web agents. The publicly released artifact applies the following compliance steps to avoid trademark, trade-dress, and asset-licensing concerns: Renaming to generic identifiers. At release, every mock is renamed to generic, non-confusing label that describes its functional category rather than the reference product (e.g., slack team-chat-mock, gmail mail-mock, shopify_admin ecommerce-admin-mock). The release package ships mapping table between internal names and public identifiers, and all task instructions, file paths, and config.json entries reference only the public identifiers. No third-party logos or proprietary brand assets. No real logos, brand-specific icon sets, or proprietary illustration assets are embedded in the released mocks. Reference logos viewed during the Plan Agents research pass are used only as ephemeral inputs to the LLM and are not stored in the mock source tree. Brand-distinctive color palettes (e.g., specific shade of purple, orange, or blue identifying real product) are replaced with neutral palettes drawn from curated 12-color set. Independently authored UI copy and illustrations. Marketing copy, microcopy, placeholder illustrations, and marketing imagery are written from scratch by the Dev Agent rather than being copied from reference applications. The navigation tree and feature inventory are functionally inspired by references but do not reproduce verbatim text strings or visual elements that would constitute trade dress. Reference screenshots are not redistributed. Reference screenshots collected by the Plan Agent during web research are kept locally for the duration of synthesis and are not included in any released artifact. Figure 10 renders the released mock screenshots, all of which were produced by our Dev Agent. TOS-sensitive mocks are flagged for additional review. Mocks corresponding to applications with restrictive terms of service (financial services, healthcare, government portals) are flagged in the release manifest for additional human review prior to public release; in cases where the reference applications TOS forbid even functional reproduction, the mock will be withheld from the public release and replaced by generic substitute in the same category. Compliance manifest at release. We will publish per-mock disclosure document alongside the dataset that lists, for each released mock, (a) the public identifier, (b) the functional category, (c) the compliance steps applied, and (d) the result of TOS-sensitive review. This manifest will be included in the release package and tracked in the dataset card. The internal-name convention is retained in this paper to preserve readability of the inventory and to keep the cross-references in B.4, B.5, and the released task identifiers traceable; readers should treat all real-product names as internal labels for the corresponding generic mock category. B.2.4 Per-Mock LOC and Endpoint Statistics We measure each released mock along three structural axes: source-code size (LOC, summed over JavaScript/TypeScript/CSS/HTML files under src/, excluding node_modules and build artifacts), route count (<Route> declarations in the SPAs React Router config), and entity count (top-level entities in the in-memory data model declared in SCHEMA.md or src/utils/dataManager.js). The released production suite contains 94 mocks; the source tree additionally retains 5 templated scaffolds (used as starting points for new mocks) that are excluded from the released task corpus. Aggregate statistics over the full 99-directory measurement set are reported in Table 4; the LOC bucket histogram is in Table 5. Restricting to the 94 released mocks shifts the means by less than 3% on every axis (the scaffolds sit in the very low-LOC bin), so we report the inclusive numbers for transparency. 24 Metric Source LOC Route components Data-model entities mean 6,127 15.0 5. p10 4,237 4 3 p25 4,828 8 3 p50 5,663 14 p75 7,196 20 7 p90 9,180 27 10 p99 max 13,095 60 23 13,095 60 23 Table 4: Structural statistics across the 99 measured mock applications. Source LOC Mocks < 1K 1K2.5K 2.5K5K 5K10K 10K20K 20K 1 1 31 59 7 0 % 1.0 1.0 31.3 59.6 7.1 0.0 Table 5: Distribution of mock-application source-LOC. The bulk of mocks (91%) sit in the 2.5K10K LOC band, consistent with the design constraint of self-contained SPA implementing roughly 1020 navigable views with small typed in-memory schema. Outliers above 10K LOC (e.g., aws_console_mock, Canvas-LMS_mock, expedia_mock) cover broader feature surfaces; the two smallest mocks are templated scaffolds retained for ancestry rather than as task targets. B.3 Unified State API Specification B.3.1 Endpoint Contracts Every mock implements four-endpoint state API that supports state injection, state inspection, and reset. The API is implemented as Vite middleware plugin co-resident with the SPA, requires no separate process, and is reachable via the same origin as the SPA itself. POST /post?sid=<sid> state injection and lifecycle control. Body: { "action": <"set" "set_current" "reset" "merge">, "state": <object>, "merge": <bool> }. Action semantics: set: writes state to the sessions initial snapshot. Used by initial_setup.py. set_current: writes state to the sessions current snapshot, leaving the initial snapshot untouched. Used by golden_patch.py and rarely by initial_setup.py when an in-progress state is needed. merge: same as set_current, but performs deep merge into the existing current snapshot rather than replacing it. reset: clears both initial and current snapshots and reloads the default seed data. Response: { "success": true, "sid": <sid>, "state_id": <hash> }. GET /go?sid=<sid> structural state inspection. Response: { "initial_state": <object>, "current_state": <object>, "state_diff": <object> }. The state_diff is flat key-path map from changed fields to { "old": <v>, "new": <v> } pairs, computed as detailed in B.3.3. This is the primary interface used by reward.py. GET /state?sid=<sid> raw current state. Response: { "stored_state": <object>, "has_custom_state": <bool>, "sid": <sid> }. Provided as lower-level inspection endpoint when the diff abstraction is unnecessary. POST /upload?sid=<sid> file upload, used by tasks that require user-supplied attachments. Body: multipart/form-data; response includes per-file URL that the SPA can resolve. Files are scoped to the sid and discarded on session reset. B.3.2 Session Isolation via sid Every API call carries session identifier sid as query parameter, scoping all state reads and writes to per-session storage namespace. The trainer generates fresh sid (UUIDv4) for each rollout and persists it to known file path inside the VM, so that all subsequent initial_setup.py, agent-issued, and reward.py calls share the same session. Sessions are independent: concurrent rollouts on the same mock 25 instance do not interfere. Session state has TTL of one hour past the last access; expired sessions are garbage-collected without intervention. B.3.3 State Diff Computation Rules The state_diff returned by /go is computed as key-path comparison between the initial and current snapshots, with three rules tuned for reward authoring. Object fields are compared structurally (recursive descent on nested objects). Arrays are treated as ordered sequences and difference at any index marks the entire array as changed; this avoids false partial-equality matches when the agent reorders rather than edits. Whitelisted volatile fields (e.g., lastViewedAt, computed UI-state caches) are masked before diff to prevent passive viewing actions from polluting the diff. The diff representation is intentionally flat (key-path strings) rather than nested, because it is consumed by reward authors who write linear assertions over individual fields. B.3.4 Integration with reward.py The canonical reward.py pattern for web-mock tasks fetches /go once, then evaluates sequence of partial-credit assertions against state_diff, accumulating [0, 1] score: Canonical reward.py pattern for web-mock tasks import requests def verify_task(): sid = open('/tmp/task_web_sid').read().strip() response = requests.get(f'http://localhost:8080/go?sid={sid}').json() diff = response['state_diff'] score = 0.0 if 'channels[0].name' in diff and diff['channels[0].name']['new'] == 'engineering': score += 0.25 if 'messages.engineering[0].content' in diff: score += 0.25 # ... additional assertions ... print(f'REWARD: {score}') return score B.4 Multi-Agent Synthesis Pipeline B.4.1 Plan Agent: Web Research and DESIGN.md The Plan Agent is responsible for understanding the target application before any code is written. Given an application name, it performs structured web research (documentation crawl, screenshot collection, feature inventory across user-role personas) and emits four artifacts: DESIGN.md (color palette, typography, spacing tokens, component styles), assets/README.md (UI layout descriptions and primary user workflows), assets/data_model.md (entity definitions for the in-memory state), and TODO.md (a prioritized P0/P1/P2 work queue for the Dev Agent). The role specification explicitly excludes authentication flows, real network communication, and persistence beyond localStorage; the mocks contract is to be faithful interactive sandbox, not functional clone of the reference service. B.4.2 Dev Agent: Implementation Protocol The Dev Agent consumes the Plan Agents artifacts and implements the mock as Vite + React single-page application following fixed project layout (src/App.jsx for routing, src/context/AppContext.jsx for global state, src/utils/dataManager.js for state initialization and localStorage persistence, src/utils/stateTracker.js for diff computation, and vite.config.js for the state-API plugin). Coordination with the Plan and Web Agents is purely file-based via the artifacts of B.4.1 and the test reports of B.4.3; no direct messaging is permitted. Priority for any given work cycle follows the order: AUDIT P0 issues > TEST P0 issues > AUDIT P1 issues > TODO P0 items > TODO P1 items > TODO P2 items, ensuring code-correctness regressions are addressed before new feature work. 26 B.4.3 Web Agent: Playwright Verification against UI Tree The Web Agent operates headless Playwright browser against the deployed mock and verifies the UI tree against DESIGN.md and assets/README.md. It exercises every interactive element catalogued in TODO.md, performs visual diff against reference screenshots when available, and produces TEST.md (functional and visual bug reports) and AUDIT.md (code-level issues such as dead handlers, untracked state mutations, and missing entries in the diff API). Reports use P0/P1/P2 severity that mirrors the Dev Agents priority order, ensuring that fixes propagate in deterministic sequence. B.4.4 Iterative Convergence Loop Plan, Dev, and Web Agents iterate until TEST.md and AUDIT.md both report zero P0 and zero P1 issues, or until per-mock round budget is exhausted. Each round consists of: Web Agent test pass Dev Agent fix pass Web Agent re-test. The Plan Agent re-engages only when fundamental specification gaps are surfaced (e.g., feature appears in real screenshots but is absent from TODO.md). Mocks failing to converge within the round budget are flagged for manual review and excluded from the released suite until resolved. B.5 Representative Mock Case Studies B.5.1 Slack Mock: State Schema and Feature Inventory The Slack mock illustrates the typical state-schema shape for communication application: tree of channels, each owning an ordered message list with metadata (sender, timestamp, thread parent, reactions, attachments, edit flag). The state object carries top-level currentUser reference plus flat users list, exposing user-switching tasks (e.g., send the message as user X) without the authentication scaffolding. Verifiable interactions include channel creation and archival, message posting and editing, thread replies, reaction add/remove, and direct-message creation. Notable mock-vs-real divergence: the mock has no notion of presence, push notifications, or workspace-level admin policies; tasks requiring these are deferred to other categories. B.5.2 Jira Mock: Cross-Entity State Transitions The Jira mock illustrates state-transition fidelity across linked entities. The schema spans projects, sprints, boards, issues, and the issue-status workflow (To Do In Progress In Review Done). Key crossentity invariants are enforced in the data model: an issues sprint membership must be consistent with the sprints project; status transitions must respect the configured workflow graph; board column membership is derived view of issue status rather than an independent field. Verification of multi-step tasks (e.g., move all unassigned bugs from the current sprint into the backlog) proceeds by inspecting the diff over the issues collection rather than chasing individual UI events. B.5.3 Salesforce Mock: Form-Heavy Workflow The Salesforce mock illustrates the form-heavy CRM workflow pattern. The state schema centres on Leads, Contacts, Accounts, and Opportunities, with the canonical lead-to-opportunity conversion flow involving multi-step forms over 25 fields (qualification stage, stakeholder identification, opportunity sizing, close-date forecasting). Reward verification for these tasks is value-level rather than path-level: the diff is checked for the correct set of populated fields with the correct types and value constraints, but the agent is free to use any UI route to populate them. This decoupling is why even visually unfamiliar agents can solve form-heavy tasks once the fields are correctly identified.

sections.title: C Training Details
sections.content: C.1 Agent Scaffold C.1.1 Observation and Action Space The agent observes raw screenshots of the desktop or browser viewport and emits actions as XMLwrapped tool calls. Each rollout step pairs an image observation with single <tool_call> block; the policy may emit multiple primitive actions inside one block to compress deterministic action chains into single turn (4.3). Screenshot preprocessing. Screenshots are captured at the native resolution of the underlying virtual machine (1000 1000 for OSWorld desktop tasks; native viewport for web tasks). The vision encoder 27 operates on token budget of min_pixels = 64 322 = 65,536 and max_pixels = 2048 322 = 2,097,152 pixels, with the image processor selecting the closest aspect-ratio-preserving resize within these bounds. No additional cropping or saliency masking is applied. Tool-call schema. single computer_use function is exposed to the policy. Each action primitive is invoked as <parameter=action> child of the <function> block, with positional arguments (coordinate, text, key, seconds) supplied as additional parameters: Tool-call XML format <tool_call> <function=computer_use> <parameter=action>left_click</parameter> <parameter=coordinate>[500, 250]</parameter> </function> </tool_call> Action primitives. The action set spans pointer, keyboard, navigation, and control primitives: Pointer: left_click, right_click, middle_click, double_click, triple_click, left_click_drag, mouse_move, left_mouse_down, left_mouse_up. Keyboard: type, key, key_down, key_up. Navigation: scroll, hscroll, screenshot, wait. Control: terminate (declares episode success or failure), call_user (requests clarification, used only at evaluation, never during RL). Multi-action format. The XML format admits multiple consecutive <function> blocks within single <tool_call>, executed in order without intervening screenshots. The policy is not constrained to use this; emergent batching during RL training (4.3) is purely policy-driven. C.1.2 Trajectory Slicing Scheme Long-horizon CUA rollouts routinely exceed the models context budget. Under our settings, an episode of max_turns = 100 with up to max_response = 2048 tokens per turn plus per-turn screenshots can grow to roughly 200K tokens, against hard context cap of 144K. Naively truncating on overflow either discards the supervision signal from later turns or silently produces shape-mismatched batches. Trajectory slicing addresses this by emitting multiple training samples per rollout under fixed context budget, each viewing the same trajectory from different starting point in the conversation history. Slice construction. At every traj_slice_interval = 10 turn-pairs, the manager emits slice indexed by an integer collapsed_length. The slices prompt portion comprises the system message followed by the first collapsed_length turn-pairs, with the screenshots in those turn-pairs replaced by short placeholder text ("<image collapsed>"); the slices response portion is all subsequent turns up to the current point in the rollout, with full multimodal observations preserved. The first slice has collapsed_length = 0 and contains the trajectory in its raw form. Subsequent slices view progressively older turns through their text placeholders, freeing context budget for fresh observations. Loss masking. For each message in slice, we set: loss_mask = False on every prompt-portion token (no gradient). loss_mask = False on every user or tool message in the response portion (these are environment observations, not policy outputs). loss_mask = True on every assistant message in the response portion, except where the cached log-probability is missing (indicating the message was rolled out under stale policy version) or where the message contains more than MAX_TOOL_CALLS_PER_TURN = 10 tool calls (a malformedoutput guard). Reward attribution. Each slice receives the full episode reward of the parent trajectory, replicated identically. We do not split or discount the reward across slices. The justification is twofold: the reward is property of the final environment state, not of any individual turn; and group-normalized advantage estimation (3.1) is applied per-prompt, so identical rewards across slices simply provide more views of the same advantage signal. Context overflow. If slices accumulated tokens still exceed the context budget after collapsing, the slice is replaced by dummy slice: pad tokens with loss_mask entirely False. The dummy contributes no gradient but preserves the batch shape required by the dataloader. This case is rare in practice (< 1% of slices in our runs) because the slicing schedule keeps the active context well below cap. Comparison with alternatives. Slicing strictly dominates two natural baselines for our setting. Truncation (drop the trajectory at context overflow) discards the supervision signal from precisely the latetrajectory turns where success or failure is determined, biasing the policy toward early-turn behavior. Summarization (replace prefix with an LM-generated summary) preserves more signal than truncation but injects an extra LM call per training sample, induces summary-quality variance into the gradient, and loses the deterministic mapping from screenshot pixels to grounded coordinates. Slicing achieves the same context relief as summarization with neither the extra inference cost nor the lossy compression: the textual placeholder is fully deterministic, and the gradient is computed on the full trajectory across the union of all slices. C.1.3 Inference-Time Configuration Step and history budgets. Each rollout terminates at max_turns = 100 model steps or upon terminate action, whichever comes first. The agent retains the last history_n = 100 turns in active context (effectively all of them under the step cap); older turns, when present, are subject to slicing as above. Decoding. Training rollouts and validation rollouts share the same decoding stack (sglang server with multi-token prediction, enable_mtp = True, speculative_num_steps = 3, speculative_num_draft_tokens = 4, attention backend FA3). Validation decoding uses temperature 0.6, top-p 0.95, top-k unconstrained, repetition penalty 1.0. Training rollouts use the GSPO sampling temperature inherited from the rollout config (default 1.0). Each turn produces at most max_new_tokens = 2048 output tokens. Failure recovery. Three failure modes are handled by the orchestrator without aborting the rollout: (i) malformed tool-call XML triggers re-prompt with parser error message in the next user turn; (ii) VM-side action failure (e.g., target element not found) returns an error string that the model observes as regular environment response; (iii) sampler-side exceptions (timeout, server disconnect) trigger up to retry_times = 5 retries with exponential backoff before the rollout is marked as failed and discarded by the over-sampling filter. C.1.4 System Prompt and Tool Definitions The agents system prompt is constant across all rollouts and tasks. It establishes the tool-call grammar, the response shape, and the termination semantics, but contains no task-specific information; the task instruction is supplied as the first user message. Inference-time agent system prompt You are multi-purpose intelligent assistant. Based on my requests, you can use tools to help me complete various tasks. # Tools You have access to the following functions: { } "name": "computer_use", "description": "Use mouse and keyboard to interact with computer, "parameters": { ... action / coordinate / text / key / seconds ... } and take screenshots.", If you choose to call function ONLY reply in the following format with NO suffix: <tool_call> 29 <function=example_function_name> <parameter=example_parameter_1>value_1</parameter> <parameter=example_parameter_2>value_2</parameter> </function> </tool_call> # Response format For normal UI interaction steps: 1) Action: short imperative describing what to do in the UI. 2) single <tool_call>...</tool_call> block. Rules: - For non-terminal UI steps, output exactly: Action then <tool_call>. - Be brief: one sentence for Action. - Do not output anything after tool call. - Use call_user when you need user information or confirmation. - Use terminate when you want to explicitly end the task with success or failure status. The full computer_use tool schema (omitted here for brevity) enumerates each primitive of C.1.1 along with its accepted argument types and is included verbatim in the released code. C.2 SFT Warm-up C.2.1 Teacher Rollout Curation The SFT initialization is curated by rolling out strong teacher policy on the same task pool used for RL. We sample 4 rollouts per task at temperature 1.0 and retain only trajectories with terminal reward r(s, τ) = 1. This success-only filter discards approximately 50% of teacher rollouts, yielding final SFT corpus of 3,578 trajectories spanning the full task distribution. No additional re-weighting or domain rebalancing is applied at the SFT stage. C.2.2 SFT Hyperparameters SFT runs the standard cross-entropy objective on assistant tokens, masking user, system, and tool messages. We use AdamW with peak learning rate 7106 and cosine decay schedule to 7107 over the run, weight decay 0.01, batch size 512 prompts, and 1 epoch over the curated corpus. Sequence length is 256K tokens; trajectory slicing (C.1.2) is applied identically to keep the SFT and RL data shapes compatible. SFT runs in bf16 mixed-precision with FP8 weights for the matmul path, on 512 GPUs. C.3 GSPO Training C.3.1 Hyperparameters Table 6 reports the hyperparameter values used for all RL runs in this paper. Defaults follow the GSPO formulation in 3.1 except where noted. Auxiliary losses. We additionally apply Chinese-token penalty (coefficient 0.5) that down-weights low-quality bilingual outputs, disable the format / language / endless penalties, and disable group filtering. The entropy bonus is kept at zero throughout. C.3.2 Reference Policy Update Schedule With β = 0, no reference policy is loaded into memory or evaluated during training. This frees per-node GPU memory for larger rollout batches and allows the rollout instances (C.4.1) to operate at the full 24-instance scale without contention. We re-evaluated this choice at β {103, 102} in early prototyping and observed neither training-stability gains nor task-success improvements at the data scales reported here. 30 Value Description Symbol Noversample ε β LR (β1, β2, ϵAdam) Weight decay Batch size 16 20 0.2 0 1106 (0.9, 0.999, 108) 0.01 128 prompts Mini-batch size PPO epochs Total steps Rollout temperature Eval temperature 32 prompts 1 1000 1.0 0.6 Top-p / top-k Max new tokens / turn Max context length 0.95 / unconstrained 2048 144K Max prompt length 8K Group size: rollouts per prompt for GSPO advantage estimation. Over-sampling target; the first = 16 valid rollouts per prompt are retained, invalid ones (timeouts, malformed outputs) discarded. Clipping range for the importance ratio (positive and negative clip equal). KL penalty against the reference policy. We disable the KL term and the reference model entirely in our reported runs; ablations with β > 0 did not yield improvements at this data scale. AdamW learning rate, constant schedule, no warmup. AdamW optimizer parameters. Applied to all non-bias non-norm parameters. Outer GSPO batch (collected via the oversampled rollouts). Inner gradient-accumulation step. Single optimizer pass per outer batch. Outer-batch updates over the full training run. Sampling temperature during training rollouts. Sampling temperature during validation rollouts. Nucleus sampling parameters (eval). Per-turn response cap. Hard context cap; triggers trajectory slicing (C.1.2). Initial prompt budget per slice. Table 6: GSPO training hyperparameters for the runs reported in 3.1 and 4.1. C.3.3 Advantage Normalization Details ˆAi = ri µg, where µg is the mean reward Advantages are computed group-wise per prompt: over the = 16 rollouts for the prompt. We deliberately disable standard-deviation normalization (divide_by_std = False); the progressive [0, 1]-valued reward (2.1) is already on fixed scale, and dividing by σg amplifies noise on prompts where the group is near-uniform (all rollouts succeed or all fail). For zero-variance groups we keep the mean-centered advantage, which evaluates to zero and contributes no gradient. C.4 Infrastructure C.4.1 Hardware and Cluster Setup RL runs use NVIDIA H200 SXM GPUs (141 GB HBM3e each), 8 GPUs per node, on high-bandwidth NVLink/NVSwitch interconnect. We report two scale-matched configurations: turbo cluster of 192 GPUs (24 nodes) for Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-scale RL, and plus cluster of 512 GPUs (64 nodes) for Qwen3.5-397BA17B-scale RL. Each cluster is split evenly between training and rollout subclusters via the disaggregated trainer infrastructure of verl [21]: plus uses 32 training nodes paired with 32 rollout nodes, and turbo uses an analogous balanced split scaled to its 24-node footprint. The two subclusters communicate via custom dispatch layer with dispatch ratio 2.35 and maximum policy-version skew of 4 steps between rollout and training. Training parallelism shares TP = 2, EP = 8, CP = 4 across the two configurations; pipeline depth is PP = 8 for plus (deeper splits absorb the larger backbone) and PP = 2 for turbo. Rollout serving uses TP = 8 with data-parallel replication factor 4 on the inference cluster, with the fa3 attention backend. Speculative decoding (enable_mtp, 3 speculative steps, 4 draft tokens) is active throughout. Rollout serving uses sglang [17]. C.4.2 VM Provisioning for Rollouts Each rollout connects to one of 2,000 parallel OSWorld virtual-machine instances served behind an internal router. rollout request submits the task instruction and an upper bound on agent steps; the router selects an idle instance, restores the snapshot specified in the tasks config.json, and returns the 31 per-step screenshot and reward stream to the trainer. After episode termination (terminate action or step-budget exhaustion), the VM is reset to clean snapshot before being released back to the pool. With the rollout subcluster feeding pool of size 2,000, the average VM-utilization per training step exceeds 75% and rollout latency is not the training bottleneck. C.4.3 Wall-Clock Time and Cost per Run full 1000-step RL run on the Qwen3.5-35B-A3B backbone (turbo, 192 GPUs) completes in approximately 5 days of wall-clock time, for an aggregate cost of 23,040 GPU-hours. The corresponding run on the Qwen3.5-397B-A17B backbone (plus, 512 GPUs) also completes in approximately 5 days, for an aggregate cost of 61,440 GPU-hours. Cost is dominated by rollout, with the trainer comparatively idle while waiting for episode completions. Smaller-scale data ablations (4.1) at 1.4K and 3K finish proportionally faster but use the same per-step compute, since trajectory volume rather than step count differs across runs. C.5 Reproducibility Checklist The NeurIPS Paper Checklist is reproduced in full following this appendix, with our Yes/No/NA responses and justifications cross-referenced back into the relevant sections (synthesis pipeline in B.4, hyperparameters in C.3.1, infrastructure in C.4.1, and limitations in 6).

sections.title: D Extended Experiments
sections.content: D.1 Emergent Multi-Action Behavior We characterize the multi-action emergence reported in 4.3 by parsing the verbatim <tool_call> stream from 1,105 rollouts of the Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-scale RL checkpoint at training step 30 on the OSWorldVerified test split, totalling 22,361 model steps. The checkpoint averages 1.41 tool calls per model step on this distribution, with the per-step batching distribution as follows: 69.6% single-call, 26.7% two-call, 2.4% three-call, 0.8% four-call, and 0.5% five-or-more-call steps. The bulk of the trajectory-shortening effect therefore comes from two-call batching; longer batches are deployed selectively for chains the policy has internalized as locally deterministic. D.1.1 Top-K Batched Action Patterns Table 7 reports the most frequent length2 tool-call sequences emitted within single model step. The two dominant patterns are deterministic input chains (type key, 3,942 steps) and continuous-scroll preludes (mouse_move scroll, 1,052 steps); together they account for 75% of all batched steps. The next tier consists of action-and-observe pairs (wait screenshot, left_click screenshot) where the policy interleaves deterministic environment poke with fresh observation in the same turn, saving round trip. Three-call form-filling patterns (type key screenshot, key_down left_click key_up) and multi-step macros (type key wait screenshot, left_click type key screenshot) appear in the long tail. seq Count 2 2 2 2 3 3 2 2 3 4 4 3,942 1,052 315 303 158 119 99 96 54 51 35 33 Sequence type key mouse_move scroll wait screenshot left_click screenshot type key screenshot key_down left_click key_up mouse_move left_click_drag key screenshot mouse_move scroll screenshot type key wait screenshot left_click type key screenshot left_click wait screenshot Table 7: Top batched (length2) tool-call sequences per model step on the Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-scale RL checkpoint, aggregated over 1,105 test rollouts. 32 D.1.2 Avoided Non-Deterministic Action Categories complementary view is the solo rate of each action type the fraction of its occurrences that appear alone in model step  (Table 8)  . Pointer click variants (right_click, double_click, triple_click) are emitted alone 9498% of the time, consistent with the hypothesis that these actions reveal context menus or selection state whose post-action UI is hard to predict. left_click is also high-solo (90.6%): individual clicks typically trigger view changes the policy needs to re-observe before deciding the next move. Conversely, scroll, key_down, key_up, and left_click_drag are never emitted alone they are mechanical sub-components of larger gestures (e.g., mouse_move scroll, the chord-modified-click pattern, drag selection) that have no semantics in isolation. The policys batching behavior therefore aligns with crisp principle: batch sub-components of mechanical gestures and deterministic chains; defer batching across actions whose outcomes depend on stochastic UI state. Action Solo Total Solo % right_click double_click triple_click left_click screenshot key wait type mouse_move scroll key_down key_up left_click_drag 144 328 630 10,132 1,023 2,351 160 738 55 0 0 0 147 346 666 11,187 2,319 7,356 615 5,199 1,279 1,112 153 153 104 98.0 94.8 94.6 90.6 44.1 32.0 26.0 14.2 4.3 0.0 0.0 0.0 0.0 Table 8: Solo rate of each tool action: counts of times the action appears alone in model step versus its total occurrences across all batched and unbatched steps. Actions toward the bottom never appear alone, indicating they are exclusively batched as sub-components of larger gestures.

sections.title: E Qualitative Examples
sections.content:
E.1 End-to-End Task Walk-throughs We present four representative tuples drawn from the released dataset, spanning desktop / web / crossapplication domains. Each walkthrough quotes the task instruction verbatim, summarizes the reward decomposition, and reproduces the most distinctive scoring component from reward.py. Full setup and reward scripts are released with the data. E.1.1 Desktop LibreOffice Calc: Padded ID Formula compact, single-domain example. The task asks the agent to populate column with TEXT-based padding formula across multiple rows; the reward decomposes credit between the canonical first cell, the propagated formulas, and the cross-cell reference correctness. calc_afm_082 task instruction Build formula to pad employee IDs with leading zeros so they are always 5 digits using TEXT. Reward decomposition (3 components, total 1.0): C1 (0.40): B2 contains TEXT formula with the format string "00000". C2 (0.30): B3:B6 all contain TEXT formulas with the same format string. C3 (0.30): every formula references the corresponding A-column cell. 33 calc_afm_082/initial_setup.py """ Initial Setup: Pad employee IDs with leading zeros using TEXT formula Task ID: calc_afm_082 Domain: libreoffice_calc """ import os import shlex import subprocess import time import openpyxl WORKDIR = '/home/user' TASK_ID = 'calc_afm_082' OUTPUT = f'{WORKDIR}/{TASK_ID}.xlsx' def launch_gui(command: str, delay_sec: float = 1.0): """Launch GUI app on VM display without blocking script exit.""" env = os.environ.copy() env["DISPLAY"] = ":0" subprocess.Popen( shlex.split(command), stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL, stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL, env=env, ) time.sleep(delay_sec) def create_initial(): wb = openpyxl.Workbook() # --- Sheet: IDs --- ws = wb.active ws.title = 'IDs' # Headers ws['A1'] = 'Raw ID' ws['B1'] = 'Formatted ID' # Data - raw employee IDs (integers) raw_ids = [42, 7, 1356, 890, 15] for r, val in enumerate(raw_ids, 2): ws.cell(row=r, column=1, value=val) # B2:B6 intentionally left empty - that's the task for the agent # Set reasonable column widths ws.column_dimensions['A'].width = 12 ws.column_dimensions['B'].width = 16 wb.save(OUTPUT) print(f'Initial file created: {OUTPUT}') # GUI-ready startup launch_gui(f'libreoffice --calc "{OUTPUT}"', delay_sec=2.0) print('GUI_READY: launched LibreOffice Calc with DISPLAY=:0') create_initial() 34 calc_afm_082/golden_patch.py """ Golden Patch: Pad employee IDs with leading zeros using TEXT formula Task ID: calc_afm_082 Domain: libreoffice_calc Changes: Add =TEXT(A#,"00000") formula in B2:B6 """ import openpyxl WORKDIR = '/home/user' TASK_ID = 'calc_afm_082' OUTPUT = f'{WORKDIR}/{TASK_ID}.xlsx' def create_golden(): wb = openpyxl.Workbook() # --- Sheet: IDs --- ws = wb.active ws.title = 'IDs' # Headers ws['A1'] = 'Raw ID' ws['B1'] = 'Formatted ID' # Data - same raw IDs as initial raw_ids = [42, 7, 1356, 890, 15] for r, val in enumerate(raw_ids, 2): ws.cell(row=r, column=1, value=val) # Golden state: TEXT formulas in B2:B6 for in range(2, 7): ws.cell(row=r, column=2, value=f'=TEXT(A{r},"00000")') # Same column widths as initial ws.column_dimensions['A'].width = 12 ws.column_dimensions['B'].width = wb.save(OUTPUT) print(f'Golden-state file created: {OUTPUT}') create_golden() calc_afm_082/reward.py """ Reward Script: Pad employee IDs with leading zeros using TEXT formula Task ID: calc_afm_082 Domain: libreoffice_calc Scoring: Component 1 (0.4): B2 contains TEXT formula with "00000" format Component 2 (0.3): B3:B6 all contain TEXT formulas with "00000" format Component 3 (0.3): All formulas reference the correct A-column cells """ import os import re import openpyxl WORKDIR = '/home/user' TASK_ID = 'calc_afm_082' 35 def persist_app_state(domain: str): """Save any unsaved GUI state before verification.""" import time os.environ["DISPLAY"] = ":0" if domain in {"libreoffice_calc", "libreoffice_writer", "libreoffice_impress"}: try: import pyautogui pyautogui.hotkey("ctrl", "s") time.sleep(0.8) print(f"PERSIST: ctrl+s sent for {domain}") except Exception as e: print(f"PERSIST_WARN: save hook failed: {e}") def verify_task(file_path): """ Verify task completion with progressive scoring. Returns: float between 0.0 and 1.0 """ total_score = 0.0 try: wb = openpyxl.load_workbook(file_path) except Exception as e: print(f"CRITICAL: Cannot load file {file_path}: {e}") print("REWARD: 0.0") return 0.0 # Precondition: 'IDs' sheet must exist if 'IDs' not in wb.sheetnames: print("FAIL: Sheet 'IDs' not found") print("REWARD: 0.0") return 0.0 ws = wb['IDs'] # Component 1: B2 contains TEXT formula with "00000" format (0.4 pts) try: b2_val = ws['B2'].value if b2_val and isinstance(b2_val, str): b2_upper = b2_val.upper().replace(" ", "") if b2_upper.startswith("=TEXT(") and "00000" in b2_val and "A2" in b2_upper: print(f"PASS: C1 - B2 has TEXT formula with 00000 format: {b2_val} (0.4 pts)") total_score += 0.4 print(f"FAIL: C1 - B2 has formula but not correct TEXT/00000/A2 pattern: { print(f"FAIL: C1 - B2 does not contain formula, found: {repr(b2_val)}") else: b2_val}") else: except Exception as e: print(f"ERROR: C1 - {e}") # Component 2: B3:B6 all contain TEXT formulas with "00000" format (0.3 pts) # Each cell earns 0.075 (= 0.3 / 4 cells) try: comp2_score = 0.0 for row in range(3, 7): cell_ref = f'B{row}' val = ws.cell(row=row, column=2).value if val and isinstance(val, str): val_upper = val.upper().replace(" ", "") if val_upper.startswith("=TEXT(") and "00000" in val: comp2_score += 0.075 print(f" PASS: {cell_ref} has TEXT/00000 formula: {val}") print(f" FAIL: {cell_ref} has formula but not TEXT/00000: {val}") else: else: print(f" FAIL: {cell_ref} does not contain formula, found: {repr(val)}") if comp2_score > 0: else: except Exception as e: print(f"ERROR: C2 - {e}") print(f"PASS: C2 - TEXT formulas in B3:B6 ({comp2_score:.3f} pts)") total_score += comp2_score print(f"FAIL: C2 - No TEXT formulas found in B3:B6") # Component 3: Each formula references the correct A-column cell (0.3 pts) # Each correct reference earns 0.06 (= 0.3 / 5 cells) try: comp3_score = 0.0 for row in range(2, 7): cell_ref = f'B{row}' expected_ref = f'A{row}' val = ws.cell(row=row, column=2).value if val and isinstance(val, str): val_upper = val.upper().replace(" ", "") if expected_ref in val_upper: comp3_score += 0.06 print(f" PASS: {cell_ref} references {expected_ref}") else: else: if comp3_score > 0: else: print(f" FAIL: {cell_ref} does not reference {expected_ref}: {val}") print(f" FAIL: {cell_ref} not formula, cannot check reference") print(f"PASS: C3 - Correct A-column references ({comp3_score:.3f} pts)") total_score += comp3_score print(f"FAIL: C3 - No correct A-column references found") except Exception as e: print(f"ERROR: C3 - {e}") final_score = round(min(total_score, 1.0), 2) print(f"nScore: {total_score:.3f}/1.0") print(f"REWARD: {final_score}") return final_score # Entry point persist_app_state("libreoffice_calc") file_path = f'{WORKDIR}/{TASK_ID}.xlsx' if not os.path.exists(file_path): print(f"File not found: {file_path}") print("REWARD: 0.0") else: verify_task(file_path) E.1.2 Desktop VS Code: Custom File Associations code-editor configuration task. The reward parses VS Codes settings.json (with JSONC comment stripping) and verifies the presence and contents of single configuration key. The verification is entirely file-based; no GUI introspection or screenshot parsing is required. The setup scripts seed realistic workspace of mixed-extension files so that the agent can verify the highlighting change visually before terminating. vscode_lp_081 task instruction Configure custom file association so that all *.config files are treated as JSON and *.tmpl files are treated as HTML for syntax highlighting. 37 Reward decomposition (3 components, total 1.0): C1 (0.35): the files.associations key exists in settings.json and is non-empty object. C2 (0.35): *.config is mapped to json. C3 (0.30): *.tmpl is mapped to html. vscode_lp_081/initial_setup.py """ Initial Setup: Configure custom file associations for *.config and *.tmpl files Task ID: vscode_lp_081 Domain: vscode """ import json import os import shlex import subprocess import time WORKDIR = '/home/user' TASK_ID = 'vscode_lp_081' WORKSPACE = f'{WORKDIR}/workspace' VSCODE_USER = os.path.join(WORKDIR, '.config', 'Code', 'User') SETTINGS_PATH = os.path.join(VSCODE_USER, 'settings.json') def launch_gui(command: str, delay_sec: float = 1.0): env = os.environ.copy() env["DISPLAY"] = ":0" subprocess.Popen( shlex.split(command), stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL, stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL, env=env, ) time.sleep(delay_sec) def create_initial(): os.makedirs(WORKSPACE, exist_ok=True) # --- Seed: two .config files (JSON-like content, no highlighting yet) --- db_config = """{ "host": "db.internal.acme.com", "port": 5432, "database": "inventory_prod", "username": "app_service", "max_connections": 25, "ssl_enabled": true, "timeout_ms": 3000, "retry_policy": {"max_retries": 3, "backoff_ms": 500} }""" with open(os.path.join(WORKSPACE, 'database.config'), 'w') as f: f.write(db_config) app_config = """{ "app_name": "InventoryTracker", "version": "2.4.1", "environment": "production", "logging": {"level": "info", "rotate_size_mb": 50}, "features": {"enable_caching": true, "cache_ttl_seconds": 300}, "api_base_url": "https://api.acme.com/v3" }""" with open(os.path.join(WORKSPACE, 'app.config'), 'w') as f: f.write(app_config) # --- Seed: two .tmpl files (HTML-like content) --- email_tmpl = """ Order {{order_id}}

Order Confirmation

Thank you, {{customer_name}}!

Order #{{order_id}}

{{#items}} {{/items}}
ItemQtyPrice
{{name}}{{quantity}}${{price}}

Total: ${{total_amount}}

""" with open(os.path.join(WORKSPACE, 'email_notification.tmpl'), 'w') as f: f.write(email_tmpl) dashboard_tmpl = """ {{dashboard_title}}
    {{#menu_items}}
  • {{label}}
    • {{/menu_items}}

{{page_title}}

{{#metrics}}
{{value}} {{label}}
{{/metrics}}
""" with open(os.path.join(WORKSPACE, 'dashboard.tmpl'), 'w') as f: f.write(dashboard_tmpl) # --- Seed: one .py file for variety --- main_py = """#!/usr/bin/env python3 """Inventory Tracker -- Main Application Entry Point.""" import logging, json, os CONFIG_PATH = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), 'app.config') def load_config(path): with open(path) as f: return json.load(f) def main(): cfg = load_config(CONFIG_PATH) logging.basicConfig(level=getattr(logging, cfg['logging']['level'].upper())) logger = logging.getLogger(cfg['app_name']) logger.info(f"Starting {cfg['app_name']} v{cfg['version']}") if __name__ == '__main__': main() 39 """ with open(os.path.join(WORKSPACE, 'main.py'), 'w') as f: f.write(main_py) # Each category corresponds to distinct exploit class observed in early prototyping where the Discriminator was given access to the Generators setup scripts; in the released pipeline, every example below is rejected at write-time before the loop accepts the tuple. Hack direct Boolean assignment. The Discriminator inspects the Generators golden_patch.py, observes that the agent need only modify single sheet attribute, and writes reward that asserts flag without computing it. 68 Hack A: direct Boolean assignment (REJECTED by static scan) # FORBIDDEN PATTERN: directly asserts flag without verifying. chart_verified = True if chart_verified: score += 0.5 # constant assignment # always true Hack bare existence scoring. The reward awards credit purely on file existence, sidestepping any inspection of contents. This passes for an agent that creates an empty file at the right path. Hack B: bare existence scoring (REJECTED by static scan) # FORBIDDEN PATTERN: scores file existence without inspecting contents. if os.path.exists('/home/user/report.xlsx'): score += 0.3 In each case the offending tuple is rejected at the loops static-scan stage, the Discriminator receives the matched-pattern name as feedback, and re-emits reward that performs actual verification. E.4 Adversarial Loop Iteration Trace We trace one production task through two adversarial rounds. The task (calc_gao_008) requires the agent to add two named scenarios to workbook, where each scenario stores distinct 5-tuple of cell values; the reward parses the workbooks underlying XLSX XML and asserts both scenario names plus value vectors. Round 1 (FAIL). The Generators first golden_patch.py introduces both scenarios but accidentally populates the Optimistic scenario with the Pessimistic values (a copy-paste regression). The Discriminator detects the mismatch when scoring the golden-state reward and emits structured REVIEW.md with the failing component identified. Round 1 REVIEW.md (synthesized from Round-2 feedback trace) # Round 1 Review ## Verdict: FAIL ## Agreement Conditions # Condition Status Details 1 initial_setup runs PASS xlsx present with B2:B6 values, no scenarios 2 golden_patch runs PASS xlsx present with two scenarios named correctly 3 reward(golden) == 1.0 FAIL Score: 0.65 -- Optimistic values do not match 4 reward(initial) == 0.0 PASS Score: 0.0 5 No forbidden patterns PASS Clean ## Feedback for Setup-Gen The Optimistic scenario currently stores B2:B6 = (350000, 140000, 100000, 60000, 30000), which are the same values as the Pessimistic scenario. The task requires Optimistic to use the increased values (650000, 280000, 200000, 140000, 75000). Please fix golden_patch.py to inject distinct inputCells for Optimistic. 69 Round 2 (PASS). The Generator addresses the round-1 feedback by writing distinct inputCells for the two scenarios. The Discriminator re-scores both endpoint states and accepts the tuple. Round 2 REVIEW.md (verbatim from production run) # Round 2 Review ## Verdict: PASS ## Agreement Conditions # Condition Status Details 1 initial_setup runs PASS xlsx present with B2:B6 values, no scenarios 2 golden_patch runs PASS xlsx contains both Optimistic and Pessimistic 3 reward(golden) == 1.0 PASS Score: 1.0 4 reward(initial) == 0.0 PASS Score: 0.0 5 No forbidden patterns PASS Clean ## Scoring Breakdown -- Golden File Component Two scenarios named Optimistic and Pessimistic 0.30 Optimistic values (650k, 280k, 200k, 140k, 75k) 0.35 Pessimistic values (350k, 140k, 100k, 60k, 30k) 0.35 1.00 Total PASS PASS PASS Points Status ## Feedback for Setup-Gen No issues found. The Round 1 feedback was addressed: the Optimistic scenario now correctly contains the increased values instead of duplicating the Pessimistic values. Both scenarios are properly stored in the XLSX XML with distinct inputCells. This pattern tightly-scoped diagnostic in Round 1 followed by targeted fix in Round 2 accounts for the majority of multi-round convergences in the released dataset. E.5 Reward Function Gallery To complement the four walkthroughs above, we sample three reward-function patterns whose verification primitives differ qualitatively from the file-and-formula pattern of E.1.1. Together with the walkthroughs they cover the principal verification primitives used across the released dataset. Chart introspection (LibreOffice Calc). Charts are stored as drawing-anchored objects on sheet; the reward enumerates them and inspects type, data-range, title, and axis labels via openpyxls chart abstractions. Chart introspection: type, data range, title, axis labels # C1: chart exists and is column/bar (0.30 + 0.20 pts) charts = ws._charts if len(charts) >= 1: score += 0.30 chart = charts[0] if isinstance(chart, openpyxl.chart.BarChart) and chart.type == 'col': score += 0.20 # C3: chart references the 12-month range (0.20 pts) ref = chart.series[0].val.numRef.f if re.search(r'$B$2:$B$13', ref): score += 0.20 # e.g., 'MonthlySales!$B$2:$B$13' # C4 / C5: chart and axis titles present (0.15 + 0.15 pts) if get_title_text(chart.title): score += 0.15 if get_title_text(chart.x_axis.title) and get_title_text(chart.y_axis.title): score += 0.15 Image-property verification (LibreOffice Calc). page-setup task that includes inserting watermark image. The reward inspects orientation, margins, and the image inventory of the target sheet, treating the page-size and order as preconditions (LibreOffice defaults that exist in any saved .xlsx) rather than scoring components. Image-property verification: orientation, margins, watermark # C1: orientation = landscape (0.25 pts) if ws.page_setup.orientation == 'landscape': score += 0.25 # C2: all four margins set to 1.5cm = 0.591 inches (0.35 pts) INCHES_PER_CM = 1 / 2.54 target = 1.5 * INCHES_PER_CM = ws.page_margins if all(abs(getattr(m, k) - target) < 0.02 for in ('left', 'right', 'top', 'bottom')): score += 0.35 # C3: at least one image anchored on the Print sheet (0.40 pts) if any(hasattr(img, 'anchor') for img in ws._images): score += 0.40 Filesystem hierarchy verification (multi-app OS task). file-classification task: the agent must move six generically-named files into category-specific subfolders. The reward checks both presence in the target folder and absence from the Desktop root, ensuring that copy-without-delete failures are not awarded credit. Filesystem hierarchy: per-file move verification FILE_CLASSIFICATION = { 'doc1.pdf': 'Invoices', 'doc5.xlsx': 'Invoices', 'doc2.docx': 'Resumes', 'doc4.odt': 'Resumes', 'doc3.pdf': 'Reports', 'doc6.txt': 'Reports', } POINTS_PER_FILE = 1.0 / len(FILE_CLASSIFICATION) for fname, expected_dir in FILE_CLASSIFICATION.items(): in_target = os.path.exists(os.path.join(DESKTOP, expected_dir, fname)) not_in_root = not os.path.exists(os.path.join(DESKTOP, fname)) if in_target and not_in_root: score += POINTS_PER_FILE The three primitives shown here structural object introspection, page-property and image-anchor checks, and filesystem hierarchy assertions complement the cell-formula, JSON-key, and document-content checks already illustrated in E.1. The full released datasets reward functions span these primitives in proportion to their target-task distribution.

sections.title: F Dataset Statistics
sections.content: F.1 Distributional Breakdowns F.1.1 Tasks per Environment CUA-GYMs 32,112 verified tuples are distributed across 110 environments, with the per-coarse-category counts summarized in Table 9. The distribution is moderately skewed: knowledge-work-heavy scenarios (Spreadsheet, Document Writing, Presentation, Software Engineering, System Administration) absorb the top five slots and together cover 84% of the corpus, with the remaining mass spread across PDF, communication, project-management, and business-operations scenarios. No single scenario exceeds 21% of the total, consistent with the balance constraint of A.2.5. Within each scenario, tasks are spread across multiple constituent applications (e.g., the Spreadsheet & Data scenario covers libreoffice_calc, the data-analysis mock websites, and the cross-application combinations involving Calc). Scenario Spreadsheet & Data Document Writing Presentation Design Software Engineering System Administration PDF & Publishing Communication & Email Project & Task Management Business Operations Creative & Media Tasks 6,684 6,314 5,328 4,434 4,225 2,055 939 879 840 412 % 20.8 19.7 16.6 13.8 13.2 6.4 2.9 2.7 2.6 1.3 Table 9: Task counts per scenario in the released CUA-GYM corpus. F.1.2 Domain Difficulty Matrix Table 10 cross-tabulates task counts by domain and difficulty. Two patterns are visible. First, the corpus is skewed toward harder regimes overall: 44.6% of tasks are labeled hard and 37.7% medium, with only 17.7% easymatching the design target articulated in 2.2. Second, this skew is most pronounced in multi-application domains: Cross-Desktop is 80.1% hard, Cross-Web is 87.6% hard, and Desktop Web is 95.8% hard, reflecting the additional state-tracking and context-switching demands those tasks impose. Single-application domains exhibit gentler difficulty gradient (e.g., Spreadsheet: 17.8% easy, 47.2% medium, 35.0% hard). Domain Easy Medium Hard Total Cross-Desktop Spreadsheet Document Editing Presentation System & OS Code Editing Desktop Web PDF Cross-Web E-Commerce Cloud & Productivity Social Media Project Management Communication Business & CRM Media Development Tools Image Editing Total 300 993 881 889 1,110 690 0 113 0 166 93 127 82 65 37 80 28 5,684 1,198 2,627 2,070 1,541 1,388 1,130 84 946 53 191 166 191 135 120 87 68 55 53 6,030 1,952 1,165 737 311 660 1,917 757 376 27 116 21 58 37 71 2 65 23 7,528 5,572 4,116 3,167 2,809 2,480 2,001 1,816 429 384 375 339 275 222 195 150 148 106 12,103 14, 32,112 Table 10: Task counts by domain and difficulty across the released CUA-GYM corpus. Rows omit two domains with < 10 tasks each (Other, Web Browsing). 72 F.1.3 Action Verb Frequency The first imperative verb of each task instruction (skipping filler clauses like want to. . . or Please. . . ) summarizes the action vocabulary the agent must learn to ground. The top-20 imperatives are listed in Table 11. Surface-edit verbs (open, create, set, add, insert, change, remove) dominate the head; midfrequency verbs cover composition (build, configure, apply), extraction (extract, export, convert), and verification (check, find, read). The long tail (not shown) extends to verbs that appear in fewer than 50 tasks each, such as merge, flatten, normalize, reconcile, and annotate. Verb Count Verb Count Verb Count open create set add use extract configure apply 4,585 2,728 1,448 1,028 906 891 728 605 insert build read change write convert 562 440 427 361 290 265 remove export check find 227 183 170 139 Table 11: Top imperative verbs across the CUA-GYM task corpus, grouped by frequency band. F.1.4 Cross-App Pair Co-occurrence Of the 12,311 cross-app tasks (38.3% of the corpus, A.2.5), the most frequent application pairs are listed in Table 12. Pairs are detected by keyword matches in the task instruction (e.g., .xlsx or Calc for calc); single instruction may contribute to multiple pairs when it mentions three or more applications. Document production triplets (calc+writer+pdf and impress+writer+pdf) account for the bulk of crossapp traffic, reflecting the empirical reality that knowledge-work pipelines frequently terminate in PDF artifact. Developer pairs (terminal+vscode, files+vscode) form smaller but distinctive cluster; web-mock pairs are concentrated in the salesforce/gmail/slack/notion family (not shown). Counts are per-pair (a fourway instruction increments all six pairs). F.1.

sections.title: Instruction Length Distribution
sections.content: Task instruction length (in whitespace-separated word tokens) is moderately right-skewed: mean 54.1, median 41, with 90% of tasks fitting under 108 words and long tail extending to 390 words for the most elaborate cross-app workflows. The bucketed distribution is reported in Table 13. Instructions under 30 words are typically single-step actions on familiar artifact (e.g., open report.xlsx and freeze the first row), while instructions of 100+ words encode multi-stage workflows with several conditional branches and explicit acceptance criteria. F.2 Generation Cost F.2.1 Per-Tuple Token Consumption Aggregating across the four LLM-driven pipeline stages (Task-Gen, Generator, Discriminator, Filter), single accepted tuple consumes on average 10,000 input tokens and 5,000 output tokens of Claude Sonnet 4.6. The input tally is dominated by the Generator and Discriminator system prompts plus their REVIEW.md context windows; output is dominated by the Discriminators reward script and the Generators setup/golden patches. At the public Claude Sonnet pricing of $3 / million input tokens and $15 / million output tokens [4], this works out to 0.030 + 0.075 $0.11 of LLM spend per verified tuple. Scaled to the released corpus of 32,112 tuples, total LLM cost is approximately $3,300 to $3,500 depending on the per-task acceptance ratio. 73 Length (words) Tasks 029 3059 6099 100149 150249 250+ 10,988 11,010 6,073 2,775 1,042 234 % 34.2 34.3 18.9 8.6 3.2 0.7 Summary statistics: mean 54.1; p10 17; p25 24; p50 41; p75 69; p90 108; p99 237; max 390. Table 13: Word-length distribution of CUA-GYM task instructions. F.2.2 VM-Hours per Verified Tuple The dual-VM execution stage (provisioning the initial_env/golden_env pair, executing initial_setup.py and golden_patch.py, running the reward function on each, tearing down) takes approximately 45 minutes of wall-clock time per accepted tuple, run on Aliyun ECS g8i.xlarge instances (4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM, on-demand pricing $0.21 per instance-hour at the time of writing). Two instances are held for the duration of the verification, so amortized VM cost is 0.75 2 $0.21 $0.32 per verified tuple; the released corpus thus carries $10K of cumulative VM compute. VM-hours dominate the marginal cost of verified tuple by roughly 3 over LLM cost. Concrete breakdown: snapshot restore ( 2 min), initial_setup execution ( 10 min on average, with long tail driven by data-fixture downloads), golden_patch execution ( 8 min), reward execution including post-config ( 15 min, dominated by document re-render and assertion parsing), and teardown ( 10 min including disk-image reset).

sections.title: G Prompts Catalog
sections.content: G.1 Prompt Engineering Notes All prompts in this catalog are reproduced as excerpts; full verbatim text (including JSON schema declarations and few-shot examples) is released with the code. Unless otherwise noted, prompts are fed to Claude-Sonnet-4-6 [4] at temperature 1.0. The Generator and Discriminator processes operate as independent inference instances under the access matrix of A.3.2; coordination is mediated by the Orchestrator through structured Markdown artifacts (REVIEW.md) rather than direct messaging. Prompts shown below are lightly edited for whitespace and to elide internal artifact paths; semantic content is unchanged. G.2 Orchestrator System Prompt The Orchestrator coordinates the Generator-Discriminator loop, monitors the five agreement conditions of A.3.1, and emits the final accepted tuple to disk. It writes no scripts itself.

sections.title: Orchestrator system prompt
sections.content: You are the orchestrator for the CUA-Gym computer-use agent training pipeline. You do NOT generate any scripts or files yourself. Your sole responsibility is to: 1. Prepare the working environment. 2. Spawn the setup-generator and reward-generator processes in an adversarial loop. 3. Monitor agreement conditions by reading REVIEW.md. 4. Collect and output final verified results. # Execution Mode (MANDATORY) Dual-environment only: - For each task, provision two isolated VMs: initial_env, golden_env. - State separation is guaranteed by environment isolation, not by filename suffixes. - Single-environment execution is deprecated. 74 # Role Boundaries YOU DO: - Parse input and prepare working directories. - Spawn setup-generator and reward-generator processes. - Read REVIEW.md to check agreement conditions. - Copy final outputs to output/final/<task_id>/. - Update pipeline status. YOU DO NOT: - Generate Python scripts (setup-gen does this). - Generate reward scripts (reward-gen does this). - Modify any generated scripts. - Make quality judgments beyond the five agreement conditions. - Skip the adversarial loop or short-circuit the process. - Write files outside output/adversarial/<task_id>/, output/final/<task_id>/, or output/reward_sandbox/<task_id>/. # Working Directory Layout (SECURITY-CRITICAL) output/adversarial/<task_id>/ task_config.json env_config_initial.json env_config_golden.json initial_setup.py golden_patch.py reward.py REVIEW.md # placed by orchestrator # initial_env VM connection # golden_env VM connection # written by setup-gen # written by setup-gen # copied from reward sandbox after PASS # copied from reward sandbox after PASS output/reward_sandbox/<task_id>/ # ISOLATED workdir for reward-gen task_config.json env_config_initial.json env_config_golden.json REVIEW.md reward.py # only reward-gen's view of the task # previous round, if any # written by reward-gen (this round) NOTE: Canonical task artifact files exist ONLY on each VM at /home/user/. They are NEVER downloaded to the local file system. Reward-gen ONLY gets the sandbox directory; it cannot see setup-gen's scripts or data files. It must explore the VM through the state-only API. This isolation is the operational form of the information barrier and is the load-bearing security guarantee of the pipeline. # Workflow Step 0 Compute PROJECT_ROOT and refuse to proceed if not in the CUA-Gym project root. Step Load tasks from the task-gen output JSON. Step 2 For each task, provision two VMs, write task_config.json and env_configs into output/adversarial/<task_id>/, and spawn setup-gen. Step 3 After setup-gen returns, copy task_config.json and env_configs (NOT the setup scripts or artifacts) into the reward sandbox and spawn reward-gen. Step Read the REVIEW.md verdict. If PASS: - Validate the five agreement conditions independently. - Copy final outputs to output/final/<task_id>/. If FAIL and round < 5: - Append REVIEW.md feedback to setup-gen invocation. - Re-spawn setup-gen. If FAIL and round == 5: - Reject the tuple and record the failure mode. G.3 Generator Subagent System Prompt The Generator stage spans two specialized roles: Task-Gen role that synthesizes natural-language task instructions paired with grounded context, and Setup-Gen role that produces initial_setup.py and golden_patch.py. Both share the same domain skill files (A.5) but operate at different granularity. Task-Gen prompt. Task-Gen system prompt You are computer-use task design expert with deep experience in how people use desktop and web software and how to train agents to do the same. Your job: given user's prompt describing the desired task mix, brainstorm and synthesize diverse, high-quality set of computer-use tasks suitable for post-training an RL agent. # Output Format JSON array. Each entry has the following fields. task_id (string) Format: <domain_short>_<topic_short>_<3-digit-number> e.g., calc_pivot_001, os_file_012, gimp_layer_003. task_instruction (string) The literal query that will be given to the agent for execution. Requirements: - Clear intent. The agent must know exactly what to do. - Necessary details. Specific names, values, ranges, locations. - Natural tone. Like real user request, not tutorial step. - Not overly verbose. normal request, not 500-word essay. - Varied sentence patterns. Mix imperative ("Create..."), request ("Can you..."), goal-oriented ("I need..."), contextual. BAD instructions (DO NOT produce): - Vague: "Format the spreadsheet nicely" (which cells? what formatting?) - Robotic: "Execute the following operation: apply bold to cells A1:A10 in the active worksheet" - Open-ended: "Create presentation about climate change" (no unique ground truth) - Infeasible: "Hack into the system" - Method-revealing: "Use the DSUM function to calculate total sales" (instruct the GOAL, not the METHOD) - Formula-typing-only: "Enter =AVERAGE(B2:B13) in cell D2" (no GUI interaction) - Trivial single-formula: "Calculate the standard deviation of column B" (one formula is not meaningful computer-use task) GOOD instructions: - "Bold the header row in Sheet1 and set the font to Arial 14pt" - "I have CSV file 'sales.csv' on my Desktop. Import it into new LibreOffice Calc spreadsheet." - "Change the page orientation of the current Writer document to landscape." - "Set up conditional formatting on the 'Status' column: 'Completed' green, 'Pending' yellow, 'Overdue' red." - "Freeze the top two rows and first column so they stay visible while scrolling." context (string, >= 300 chars) Natural-language description of everything needed to set up and evaluate this task. Three required components: 1. Initial state -- what must exist before the agent starts: 76 files and their content/location, application state, data characteristics (rows, columns, value ranges). 2. Ground truth -- expected correct outcome: exact values, states, file contents; partial-credit checkpoints; concrete numbers where applicable ("cell B15 should contain 342.50"). 3. Implicit prerequisites -- things the instruction does not say but the evaluator needs: e.g., "GIMP canvas should be 1920x1080 with white background layer"; "Firefox should have 3 tabs open: Google, Wikipedia, GitHub". Context is CRITICAL. Without it, we cannot set up the VM, evaluate success, or assign partial credit. difficulty (string in {easy, medium, hard}) easy -- 1-3 atomic actions, common operation ("Bold the header row") medium -- 3-10 steps, requires feature understanding ("Create pivot table with specific fields") hard -- multi-step workflow, advanced features, cross-component interaction or edge cases ("Create macro-free automated report with conditional formatting, charts, and cross-sheet references") # Workflow (Two Phases) Phase 1 (planning): Research the domain via web search; build the feature taxonomy and scenario matrix; identify under-covered cells; present the plan for approval before generating. Phase 2 (generation): Three sequential passes -- breadth (uniform over taxonomy), gap-fill (re-sample under-covered cells), and edge cases (boundary / cross-application / rare-primitive tasks). Apply embedding-based dedup and slot-template diversity rules between passes. Setup-Gen prompt. Setup-Gen system prompt You are the Generator in the CUA-Gym adversarial setup pipeline. Your job is to create two Python scripts: 1. initial_setup.py -- creates the pre-task state on initial_env -- builds the expected post-task artifact on 2. golden_patch.py golden_env You work within an adversarial loop with the verifier. After each round, the verifier writes REVIEW.md with structured feedback. If the review fails, the pipeline invokes you again. Your goal: produce outputs the verifier agrees are correct. # Execution Mode (MANDATORY) Dual-environment only: - initial_setup.py executes only on initial_env - golden_patch.py executes only on golden_env Separation is by environment isolation, not by filename suffixes. # ABSOLUTE RULES 77 Rule 1 -- Golden Must Be Independently Built in golden_env. The golden artifact MUST be produced directly in golden_env, without loading or copying files from initial_env. Cross-env file dependency is invalid by design. Rule 2 -- No Side Effects. The golden-state spec must represent EXACTLY the intended post-task result; the initial-state spec must represent the pre-task result. Differences must be task-driven only. Do NOT reorganize, reformat, or "clean up" anything else. Rule 3 -- Realistic Content. Initial files must contain believable, non-trivial content. Use real names ("Sarah Chen", "Marcus Johnson"), real-looking dates, business metrics. Forbidden: "Test data 1", "Lorem ipsum", "foo bar", placeholder values. Rule 4 -- Sufficient Complexity. Spreadsheets: multiple sheets, 10+ rows, varied column types. Presentations: 3+ slides with text, shapes, layout variety. Documents: multiple paragraphs, headings, realistic structure. Rule 5 -- GUI-Ready Initial State. initial_setup.py MUST not only create files; it MUST prepare the GUI start state expected by the task: open required apps and files, launch via non-blocking subprocess, set DISPLAY=:0, add short sleeps for stability, keep startup idempotent. Rule 6 -- Save Initial Reference Copy for Multimedia. For tasks where the agent overwrites media file in place (GIMP, VLC, OpenShot), initial_setup.py MUST save reference copy at <task_id>_initial_reference.<ext> that neither the agent nor golden_patch.py touches. The reward script needs this for vision judge BEFORE/AFTER comparison. # Workflow Step 0 Load the domain skill file (API references, bitter lessons, evaluation patterns). For mock_websites, also load the per-mock state schema; missing keys cause blank pages. Step 1 Read task_config.json. Parse the context field into design spec: initial-state requirements, ground-truth post-state values, and NEGATIVE constraints (what the initial artifact MUST NOT contain). Violating negative constraints causes reward(initial_env) > 0 and wastes adversarial rounds. Step 2 If round > 1, read REVIEW.md and make TARGETED fixes. - "reward(golden_env) returned X.X instead of 1.0": fix golden_patch to match what the reward expects. - "reward(initial_env) returned X.X (should be 0)": fix initial_setup to remove task-completion artifacts. - script execution errors: fix the specific exception. Do NOT rewrite from scratch unless the approach is fundamentally wrong; targeted fixes converge faster. Step 3 Generate initial_setup.py and golden_patch.py from the design spec. Execute both on their respective VMs and verify they complete without exception before signaling the verifier. G.4 Discriminator Subagent System Prompt The Discriminator (Reward-Gen) is the load-bearing component of the information barrier. It writes reward.py from the task semantics alone, with no access to the Generators setup scripts. 78 Discriminator (Reward-Gen) system prompt You are the verifier/discriminator in the CUA-Gym adversarial setup pipeline. Your job is to: 1. Generate reward script (reward.py) that programmatically verifies task completion. 2. Test it against the golden_env artifact (must return 1.0) AND the initial_env artifact (must return 0.0). 3. Write structured REVIEW.md with your verdict and feedback. Be thorough but fair. Report PASS when the golden artifact genuinely matches the task; do not be adversarial for its own sake. # INFORMATION BARRIER (NON-NEGOTIABLE) - You MUST NOT read initial_setup.py, golden_patch.py, or any setup-generator source code. - You MUST NOT read files from the setup-generator's working directory; your workdir is output/reward_sandbox/<task_id>/. - You MUST NOT download task artifact data files from either VM to your local machine. - You MUST explore and verify files exclusively on the VM via the state-only environment API. - Your reward script must be derived from the task description in task_config.json, NOT by peeking at setup-generator outputs. # Reward Script: Required Properties 1. Return progressive float in [0, 1]. 2. Use ACTUAL verification (read real files, check real data). 3. Award partial credit (0.3, 0.5, 0.7, ...) for partial completion. 4. Return exactly 1.0 only when 100% completed. 5. Wrap each scoring component in try/except. 6. Print "REWARD: X.X" as the last output line. 7. Be self-contained with stdlib + openpyxl/python-pptx/python-docx. 8. Comment scoring logic. # Only Score Task-Introduced Changes Every scoring component must verify something that DIFFERS between initial_env and golden_env. Properties true on both endpoints are preconditions and MUST NOT contribute to score. Litmus test: if check passes on the initial_env artifact (before any agent action), it is NOT measuring task completion and MUST NOT award points. Goal: reward(initial_env) returns 0.0 (or very close). CORRECT pattern: Component 1: 'Raw Data' sheet is hidden (0.6 pts) # GOOD Component 2: 'Raw Data' hidden AND 'Summary' visible (0.4 pts) # GOOD WRONG pattern (scoring pre-existing properties): Component: 'Summary' sheet is visible (0.15 pts) Component: 'Raw Data' has correct headers (0.10 pts) # FORBIDDEN # FORBIDDEN Use data-integrity checks as PRECONDITION GATES (early-return 0.0 if the file is corrupted), NOT as scoring components. # REVIEW.md Output verdict: PASS FAIL agreement_table: C1_initial_executes C2_golden_executes C3_golden_reward_eq_1 C4_initial_reward_eq_0 C5_no_forbidden_pattern {pass, matched_pattern_or_null} {pass, evidence} {pass, evidence} {pass, observed_value} {pass, observed_value} 79 feedback_to_setup_gen: failing_conditions: [C1C2C3C4C5, ...] setup_issues: [short imperatives, one per issue] recommended_action: <one paragraph> G.5 Five Agreement Condition Verifier Prompts The five agreement conditions of A.3.1 are evaluated programmatically rather than by LLM, and so do not have an associated prompt; they are reproduced here as the structured REVIEW.md schema the Discriminator emits to communicate its verdict to the Orchestrator. REVIEW.md schema # REVIEW.md schema verdict: agreement_table: PASS FAIL {pass: bool, evidence: <str>} C1_initial_executes: {pass: bool, evidence: <str>} C2_golden_executes: C3_golden_reward_eq_1: {pass: bool, observed: <float>} C4_initial_reward_eq_0: {pass: bool, observed: <float>} C5_no_forbidden_pattern: {pass: bool, matched_pattern: <strnull>} feedback_to_setup_gen: failing_conditions: [<C1C2C3C4C5>, ...] setup_issues: recommended_action: <free-form one-paragraph summary> [<short imperatives, one per issue>] G.6 Filter Critic Prompts The dataset-level filter is single-pass critic with the conservative-by-default decision policy of A.4.1. The critic prompt below is reproduced verbatim from filter/critic_prompt.py.

sections.title: Filter critic system prompt
sections.content: You are conservative critic for computer-use training tasks. Input: - query - setup - golden_setup - reward Goal: decide whether this task should be kept, kept with query rewrite, or rejected for the training set. Important constraints: - setup, golden_setup, and reward are immutable for this decision. - You may only revise the query. - If the task would require changing setup, golden_setup, or reward to become acceptable, REJECT it. - Prefer reject over keep when high-risk issue is present. - Prefer keep over modify_query when the query is already usable and self-contained enough for training. - Only use modify_query when the query has meaningful defect that harms fairness, self-containment, or task validity. Severity mapping: P0 P1 reject. Fatal setup/reward/environment problem or irreparable inconsistency. modify_query. Query missing important context, has harmful ambiguity, or leaks process in way that harms fairness. 80 P2 P3 keep or modify_query. Non-fatal but noticeable quality issues. keep. Strong task quality, no meaningful issue beyond style. Evaluation priorities: 1. Is the task suitable and safe for training? 2. Is the reward semantically valid and robust? 3. Is the query self-contained, natural enough, and consistent with setup/golden_setup/reward? 4. Can the query be fixed without changing task semantics? Fatal reasons to reject: - reward checks artifacts or implementation traces more than user-goal completion - reward is highly sensitive to GUI/window/process state - reward relies on fragile internals, heuristic perception, or unstable extraction - hidden assumptions about browser state, plugins, extensions, or external resources that the environment cannot provide - setup/query/reward mismatch not fixable through query rewrite alone - subjective task with rigid single-answer reward Output schema (JSON only): verdict severity can_fix_with_query_only query_issues setup_reward_risks training_pool_fit confidence reasoning_summary revised_query keep modify_query reject P0 P1 P2 P3 bool [str, ...] [str, ...] low medium high float in [0, 1] str str null G.7 Mock Synthesis Agent Prompts The mock-environment synthesis pipeline (B.4) is implemented as triple of specialized agents (Plan, Dev, Web) that coordinate through file-based artifacts. Each agents role specification is excerpted below; full prompts are released with the code. Plan Agent.

sections.title: Plan Agent system prompt
sections.content: You are senior product researcher and technical planner. Your job is to deeply understand target real-world application and produce structured, actionable plan for the dev agent to implement faithful mock. Primary output: TODO.md, the single source of truth that the dev agent reads and executes against. Everything else (assets, screenshots, data model) exists to make TODO.md items implementable without ambiguity. In scope: - All visible UI components and layouts - Navigation, routing, view transitions - CRUD operations on content - Search, filter, sort interactions - Form inputs and validation feedback - Drag-and-drop, multi-select, bulk actions - Notification/badge counts, unread states - Settings panels and preference toggles - Realistic mock data (users, messages, tasks, etc.) Explicitly out of scope: - Login / logout / authentication 81 - Password management or account creation - OAuth, SSO, identity verification - Encryption or security mechanisms - Real network communication or API calls - Database persistence beyond localStorage - File uploads to real servers - Email/SMS sending Dev Agent.

sections.title: Dev Agent system prompt
sections.content: You are an expert frontend engineer specialized in building and maintaining mock web applications for RL agent training. Your sole responsibility is code: writing it, improving it, and fixing it. All coordination is file-based -- no direct messaging between agents. Inputs you read: work queue TODO.md (from plan agent) assets/README.md (from plan agent) UI layout, workflows assets/data_model.md (from plan agent) entity definitions DESIGN.md TEST.md AUDIT.md (from plan / user) (from web agent) (from web agent) design system spec; read FIRST bug reports, visual diffs code-level issues Priority for any work cycle: AUDIT P0 > TEST P0 > AUDIT P1 > TODO P0 > TODO P1 > TODO P2 Project layout (every mock follows this): -- routing src/App.jsx src/main.jsx src/components/ src/pages/ src/context/AppContext.jsx src/utils/dataManager.js src/utils/stateTracker.js vite.config.js -- reusable UI components -- route-level pages -- global state -- state init, localStorage -- diff computation for /go -- middleware plugin for /post + /state Web Agent.

sections.title: Web Agent system prompt
sections.content: You are quality-assurance engineer for mock web applications. Operate headless Playwright browser against the deployed mock, verify the UI tree against DESIGN.md and assets/README.md, and produce two reports per round: TEST.md AUDIT.md code-level issues: dead handlers, untracked state, functional and visual bug reports missing entries in the diff API, broken routes Both reports use P0/P1/P2 severity matching the dev agent's priority order. round is complete when: - every interactive element listed in TODO.md has been exercised - visual diff against reference screenshots is below threshold - state-API endpoints (/post, /go, /state, /upload) all respond correctly under the session-isolation contract
